document_ratings.weight_rationale: Canonical victory bulletin; highest rhetorical prestige in the corpus.
document_ratings.rationale: Austerlitz strongly enacts glory-destiny logic and the army-as-sacred-collective frame; enemy is obstacle rather than death-bearer; soldier sacrifice is present but thin in direct expression.

document_ratings.weight_rationale: Highest metaphor density bulletin (8 instances); the one text where soldier death and sacrifice are most directly confronted.
document_ratings.rationale: Eylau directly addresses mass casualties and converts them into sacrifice for glory and the Emperor; the closest Napoleon's bulletins come to explicit sacrificial economy logic.

document_ratings.weight_rationale: The 29th Bulletin is the sole public acknowledgment of catastrophic loss; rhetorically important as a limit case.
document_ratings.rationale: The 29th Bulletin acknowledges catastrophe but reframes it as destiny; the enemy (Russian winter/army) is more explicitly death-bearing here than elsewhere in the corpus.

document_ratings.weight_rationale: Remaining bulletins (Jena, Berlin, Friedland, Wagram, Bautzen, Austerlitz-aftermath) averaged as a group; individually thin.
document_ratings.rationale: The remaining bulletins show glory-destiny and army-body framing but are individually thin on sacrifice and enemy-death logic.

limitations: Corpus is limited to official military bulletins — a propaganda genre that systematically suppresses loss and dissent.
19 total instances across 9 documents is a thin evidence base; several clusters have 0 or 1 instances.
Napoleon-04-empire-order and napoleon-06-enemy-obstacle have 0 instances in current review; those dimensions are under-evidenced.
Source-language (French) glosses have been reviewed but translation ambiguities remain in the candidate domain assignments.
Historical alignment notes require publication-grade citations before final scoring.
